sentences.text: Probably from the end of July until Christmas Day.
Well, the factory where we work, it was a very-- place where we was horrible workers, because we tried to destroy everything we can.

Because if they told us it was element we have to work with it, and we have to put screws and other kind of wires in this element, if we was told it has to be done at the right side, if we can, we did as many on the left side as it was possible.
Other things we did, we goes to the washroom, and we throw as many pieces into the washroom as we can.
Unfortunately, the washroom was, you know, flooded, and they find out this.
They want to know who did this, and quite a few pay big price for this.
It was a very clean factory, and it was like a chain work.
Maybe ten of us sitting in one line, and you do this, this, and the next did something what he has to do.
There was Germans foremans who was teaching us.
You also have very, very fine machinery to making coals in certain element.
And we start-- we got to get up 4:30 in the morning to get ready, before they count us all together.
And we start at 7 o'clock in the factory with no food at all, because we only get food at night, after our work.
We have 12 hours work to do. 10 o'clock, we got some hot water or some kind of a tea.
We also have our lunch break, if you have something to eat, if you save something from the night before.
Otherwise, we wasn't hurt there, or any way.
We was normally treated-- if you do your work.
If they find out you did something wrong, you been taken away.
Or if you become sick, you are also taken back to Auschwitz and killed.
Yes.
We all have our friends.
We usually have just like a sister whom you have all your food together and next to your bed, and you give them all the messages you want to give them, in case one of us dies.
And if one would live it through, let them give the message for what happened and where we were.
And we was very, very close, loving friendship with quite a few girls from my city.
There was about three from my city.
One's name was Fischer.
Her father was a baker.
Was also Magda.
[LAUGHS] Same age.
And there was another girl.
I don't remember her name.
She was a very [NON-ENGLISH], a very orthodox girl, but I do not know her name.
But she was very, very pretty and [INAUDIBLE], and very, very elegantly dressed.
Famous for her elegantly dress-- though she wasn't especially rich.
But her mother have a salon, was making clothing.
And this was the other one from my city.
There was a third one.
This was a family, also was a family of five sisters.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Their father also have a baker's shop.
But one day, four of the sisters has been taken, and they only left there one.
And I do believe none of the four ever came back.
No.
I don't know, really, their names.
Well, we was talk about what we eat, because we were so hungry, how our mothers cook.
Talking about our homes and our life.
And we was mostly talk about it and hoping to be home to the next holiday, and what we will do if, once more, we go home.
Well, first of all, I was very, very mad.
I can't help to say it-- I was very mad.
I decided, if I once more could pay back for certain people, I do my best.
And I would do my best.
Well, people who hurt us, people who robbed us, people who write horrible articles in the paper against the Jews.
They are making comics and things like this, and this was the neighbors.
And the media, who was very, very, for the Germans, and other people who might decide we just no people at all, and mostly we was killed Jesus.
That's the reason we are treated like this.
And we just want to pay them back.
[LAUGHS]
Same way.
Same way.
I would really talk against them, and I decided many of them deserved that-- the de Which they get, too.
Quite few was getting hanged-- and-- mostly hanged-- in Germany for things they have done with children.
And they just kill them all, throw them to the [NON-ENGLISH] in life, and people like this.
Also, I find great anti-Semitism in the churches, who was really preaching against the Jews.
They do nothing to save us-- nothing.
It was-- Hungary is a big Catholic country.
So they pretty well agree, quietly and openly, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I wouldn't feel sorry for them.

Because what I saw, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the things what did happen in Auschwitz, we-- before the bomb in Reichenbach, we got the last shipment from Auschwitz, which was the people who was working in the crematorium.
And they was promised by the German, they're not going to kill them, because they was working with them.
Not in their heart, they just want to save their life.
And they arrive in Auschwitz about-- no, in Reichenbach-- about two weeks before Christmas.
And when we asked them what did happen in Auschwitz, they told us for the last three months, Auschwitz was burning and never, ever stop.
So they burn everyone whom they could.
And I think, finally, the Russian was so very close, they have to close Auschwitz somehow.
And this how about this eight or 10 girls was taken to Reichenbach.
They was all Polish.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we have no sorrow to the Germans this time.
All we was wishing to get all they deserve.
Yes.
I feel much better.
I was very hungry, but somehow I was hoping to survive, and I try to work for this.
I know one survive.
The other, we kind of-- you know, quite often, we was separated in the wagons, and I don't know whether they do survive or not.
Christmas Day, the bomb was coming.
We didn't get the bomb when the Jews was working, though the other side, which get the bomb, was also all people who was working, wasn't German.
They was the same, but maybe wasn't Jewish.
And some German was working, also, in this factory.
Most of the one who was working there, they came back from the war amputated with one legs, maybe even both of their legs.
And they put them back to work, whatever they can do.
The bomb fall just about where we were, but still not over our head, but maybe to the next and the next building.
It was a big glass ceilings, big lights and everything, and it did good damage on this factory.
It could never be used again, this where that fell.
And it also damaged many of the other houses which look like houses, normal family houses, and they was all factories.
And it seems to be, whoever did the bombing knew what was they doing.
The prisoners, we cannot go back to our own barrack, because it was bombed.
So we run away, because we was told, go down in the basement.
By this time, nobody care, at least not the Jews, because we was also hoping that's the end of the war, maybe.
And it was almost, some places.

So we run on the street, as many as we could, looking for our friend who might stop and can't work, because some got shock over the bomb, when we tried to hold as many as we could and run on the street.
We was catched after this, because we have no place to go, and some were-- no food, nothing.
And then, we was also taken to the [NON-ENGLISH].
What was it like there?
We have no more beds there.
We go back to the floor, and as many in one room, maybe, as many could.
We do get some food, and it wasn't long.
The bombing really never stop in Reichenbach.
It was keep on going and going.
And this was the time they transferred us to another factory, called Porta, about two weeks later.
Another radio factory, which was also bombed.
After we worked there only one week, this factory was also bombed and flooded.
After this, we was taken from there, because I think-- I don't know why, but maybe the Russian came, or whoever.
But they emptied the city with prisoners and everything.
From Porta we go into Freidenen, I think it was the place.
We wasn't work there anymore.
By foot.
Four, five days.
January in '45.
Well, we got, this time, clothing, and we also have a coat, a good coat and things, because we was working for them.
And the one who could walk was doing.
At night, we sleep in barracks-- not in barracks, but wherever the horses are.
Some place, at night, we wasn't in the open air, until we reach it.
The one who can't walk has been killed.
My physical condition was not bad.
Nothing.
Nothing.
We was just thinking to able to walk until night come and we could stop.
There is not much time to think, because if you're not going with the group, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you're just killed.
And the one who couldn't walk, we try to hold it by both hand, and we help a lot of each other.
But this time, the Germans wasn't as bad as before.
They kind of realize that they're losing the war.
They even encourage us, start to walk.
They said, it's only two more kilometer, only three more.
Don't throw out to kill yourself, because you're going to get food and a warm place to sleep, if you could do the walking.
They even, the one who was sitting down as can't walk, and they really try to encourage you to do it.
But the one who can do it, can do it.
And one funny things happened one day-- five girls, this time we was walking, and the Germans always has a big suit, and they gave this to one of the five girls to carry their suit.
And this day, those five girls nicely disappeared with their suitcases.
[LAUGHS] And they did not catch them.
They promised us everything-- double bread, everything-- where the five girls gone?
See, the girls known the place.
And we don't know where they-- even if we know, who would ever say?
And we was happy for them.
And after this-- [SIGHS] after this-- When you got to Freidenen, what happened there?
Freidenen, there was a camp where was other girls.
And it wasn't a crematorium there.
We was there for a few days, just to have a rest.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe four, five days, and after this, we have been gone to the trains again, with the open trains, until we reach Salzwedel.
And our trip in the open train was two to three weeks.
This is the worst time in our life.
It was as bad as Auschwitz, because this time, we was in the winter.
We was very, very sick this time in our bodies, full of louses, full of sickness.
And the cold didn't help us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] With these wagons, there was the snow, rain, whatever.
And all we have is a blanket all over our head, and a group hanging together-- usually, as many as you were-- to keep you cold-- to warm.
And between the group, every morning, quite a few dead.
On the morning when we was waked up-- we wasn't waked up, because we wasn't laying down.
We was sleeping by standing.
They just ask you how many dead on the morning, and that has to be thrown out from the wagon.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this time, we see the bombing.
We was close to Hanover and other places, and Hanover was burning.
And our wagon was moving with this bomb.
And we was, again, wishing more bomb coming.
We don't care this time, whatever would happen.
Many girls just ask for more bomb or death, whatever, until we reach Salzwedel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the last of my trip.
There was again the camp.
In this camp, we was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] pretty well, the weather turned better, because it was April.
And we was finally freed by the American.
I would thing two weeks.
No.
No, we didn't work there.
Nobody work there anymore.
There was a factory.
The girls has to do something with sunflower seed, making oil or something, and they eat the sunflower seed.
Every girl's pocket, it they have, was full of the sunflower seed, and this kept them healthy.
And we got, also, some other food, like bread or margarine.
The condition there, the same condition.
You sleep on the floor, and you get some food, and you're waiting for a miracle that would happen.
But we knew that the war is pretty well over, because on the houses-- this is, again, a small place.
You could see, even from the camp, which was near to, they all have a white sheet in their windows and things.
So they was waiting, and the American was there.
The liberation was one soldier come somehow, and he was amazed what he was seeing.
And some girl was at the fence at the camp.
And he asked them, what is in here?
Because he saw how we was dressed, and the girls was on the front of the barracks.
And somehow, they make him understood, this is a concentration camp.
And he only say to these girls-- if they understood it, good-- this.
I'll be back.
And it was daytime, the afternoon, when all the American was coming with cars, [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know how many of them.
And we know, this is it.
We just felt it.
This is the Americans.
And we all run out, as many as they could, to look what's happening.
And the lager fuhrer went right to the doors, just like he was expecting this would happen.
And it did happen, and he said, I am the lager fuhrer in this camp.
And this all he has to say, and he was shot.
I do not know his name.
But the girls in the camp want to save this man's life, with another one or two.
They claim, when they hear what was happening with us and the rest of the people, they said they had it very good, because he never killed anybody.
And they claimed they wasn't have this horrible time what we did have, and they was told they might be free in life.
And about 10 days later, all of us was taken from the camp to hospitals.
I don't know.
Nothing.
I know nothing.
I-- I-- I don't know.
Yes.
Yes.
It was just one German that was shot.
Many of us was shot on the road for nothing, just because they can't walk.
They get no help.
But these girls say, this man shouldn't be shot.
And we have the joy, about 10 days later, to see this lager was all burned by the American.
We was also told it was all undermined.
So if they didn't come at time, maybe a week later, no good how the German was look so good, but we would be all killed.
All of us was taken to the hospitals.
I was sick.
I got the food, all kind of a food, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] water, milk, a chocolate, meat.
And we all get sick of this.
This was a big mistake they did.
We all got kind of sick in our stomach, sort of a typhus type of a sickness.
But I wasn't really having the typhus very, very hard.
But you get feverish, very sick.
You practically have to learn not to touch this food again.
You just have to eat very little, and very light food.
Yes.
